skill.name: clean-code
skill.description: Clean code guidance for naming, functions, comments, error handling, formatting, and common code smells.
skill.summary: Apply practical clean code principles to make software easier to read, review, and maintain.
skill.author: Benny9193

security_audit.summary: AI review found that the static findings are false positives from Markdown code fences, TypeScript examples, and substring matches in SKILL.md. No executable scripts, network access, credential handling, prompt injection attempt, or confirmed malicious behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged TypeScript fenced examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These are documentation examples in SKILL.md, not executable scripts or runtime commands, so no command execution path was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains Markdown fenced TypeScript examples and no executable helper files. The flagged backticks are documentation delimiters or template literal examples, not shell execution.

low_findings.title: Illustrative Temp Directory Usage Only
low_findings.description: The /tmp references appear inside an example about avoiding flag arguments. They are not invoked by the skill and no filesystem writes are performed by the package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only temp path references are in TypeScript documentation examples. No script, instruction, or tool wrapper executes these examples.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts do not correspond to cryptographic operations in the reviewed file. The cited lines contain metadata or clean code checklist text, with no hashing or encryption calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations do not include MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar cryptographic APIs. The pattern appears to be a substring match rather than a semantic finding.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Pattern Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alerts do not map to commands that inspect the host or environment. The cited sections discuss function arguments, null handling, code smells, and a checklist.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No uname, whoami, id, hostname, or environment discovery command appears at the cited lines. The surrounding content is instructional prose and inert TypeScript examples.

content.value_statement: Unreadable code slows reviews, onboarding, and bug fixes. This skill gives concise clean code rules and examples for naming, functions, comments, errors, formatting, and code smells.
actual_capabilities: Explains naming practices for variables, functions, and booleans.
Shows how to split functions by responsibility and reduce parameter complexity.
Compares unclear comments with self-explanatory code and useful comments.
Guides error handling choices around null returns, null parameters, and Result types.
Lists common code smells and practical refactoring responses.
Provides a pre-commit checklist for clean code review.
limitations: Provides general guidance, not language-specific lint rules for every stack.
Does not execute formatters, tests, or static analysis tools by itself.
Examples focus mainly on TypeScript-style syntax.
Does not replace project-specific architecture or security review standards.

use_cases.title: Review a Pull Request for Readability
use_cases.description: Use the checklist to identify unclear names, long functions, flag arguments, and avoidable null handling before merging.

use_cases.title: Refactor Legacy Code in Small Steps
use_cases.description: Apply naming, extraction, early returns, and code smell guidance without changing behavior in a large codebase.

use_cases.title: Teach Clean Code Practices
use_cases.description: Use the examples to explain practical maintainability rules during onboarding, mentoring, or team standards discussions.

prompt_templates.title: Check Basic Readability
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small function or file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this code for clean code basics. Focus on names, function size, comments, and obvious code smells. Give a short prioritized list.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Function Design
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate refactoring of functions with mixed responsibilities.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these functions using clean code principles. Suggest clearer responsibilities, better parameters, and simpler control flow without changing behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Pull Request Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Structured review before a merge decision.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the clean code checklist to review this pull request. Separate blocking maintainability issues from optional improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Legacy Refactor
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced refactoring plan for risky or older code.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a safe clean code refactoring plan for this legacy module. Identify code smells, behavior-preserving steps, test needs, and review order.

output_examples.input: A function named process that validates input, writes a record, sends email, and logs activity.
output: Rename the function to describe the main business action.
Extract validation, persistence, notification, and logging into separate functions.
Add tests before refactoring because the current function combines several behaviors.

output_examples.input: A review asks whether a boolean flag parameter should stay in a file creation function.
output: Replace the flag with separate functions for the two behaviors.
Name each function after the result it creates.
Keep call sites explicit so reviewers do not need to inspect the implementation.

output_examples.input: A team wants a clean code checklist before committing changes.
output: Check whether names are descriptive and searchable.
Confirm each function has one clear responsibility.
Remove dead code and replace magic values with named constants.

best_practices: Apply the checklist to small changes before asking for review.
Prefer behavior-preserving refactors when improving legacy code.
Use project conventions and automated formatters alongside this guidance.
anti_patterns: Do not rename or split code without understanding existing behavior.
Do not use clean code rules to make broad unrelated refactors in one change.
Do not replace domain-specific team standards with generic examples.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps review and improve code readability, maintainability, naming, function design, comments, formatting, and common code smells.

faq.question: Does it run automated refactoring tools?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and review criteria, but it does not run formatters, linters, tests, or codemods.

faq.question: Which languages does it cover?
faq.answer: The examples use TypeScript-style syntax, but the principles apply to many programming languages.

faq.question: Can it replace code review?
faq.answer: No. It supports code review by highlighting maintainability concerns, but human reviewers still need project context.

faq.question: Is it safe to use on private code?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation-only and contains no executable scripts or network behavior. Follow your normal data handling policies.

faq.question: How should teams use it effectively?
faq.answer: Use it as a shared checklist for small, focused improvements during reviews, refactors, and onboarding.
